title: What is left when the image is gone
testCase: Do both images have an alt that says what is on them?
blocks.0.text: Two images. One with an empty alt, the other with alt="photo". Both show up fine. Everything works.
blocks.2.text: Change both paths to something that does not exist — say /uroci/nothing.jpg. Run it. Watch carefully.
blocks.3.text: This is what a person sees when the image does not load. One of your images leaves nothing behind — an empty gap, as if nothing was ever there. The other says "photo". Thanks, very helpful.
blocks.4.text: And images fail to load more often than you think: slow mobile data, a deleted file, a wrong path, images turned off. In those moments the alt is the ENTIRE content of the image. It is not a fallback. It IS the image, in words.
blocks.5.text: Write the alt the way you would describe the picture to someone over the phone.
blocks.6.text: And for the one who will never see it
blocks.7.text: A blind visitor uses a screen reader — a program that reads the page aloud. When it reaches an image, it has nothing to say. Unless you told it what to say.
blocks.8.items.0: alt="" → the reader stays silent and moves on. Sometimes that is right (see below), but more often it was just forgotten.
blocks.8.items.1: No alt at all → the reader reads out the file name. "bike2 dot jpg". That is the worst case.
blocks.8.items.2: alt="Black bike, side view" → the person understands what you are selling.
blocks.9.text: Tens of thousands of people have serious visual impairments. They shop online. The only thing keeping them from understanding your listing is you.
blocks.10.text: How to write a good alt
blocks.11.items.0: Say what IS SHOWN, not what the file is. "Black bike next to a tree", not "photo1".
blocks.11.items.1: Do not start with "Image of…" — the reader has already said it is an image.
blocks.11.items.2: Short and to the point. One sentence is enough.
blocks.11.items.3: Context matters. The same photo in a bike listing and in a repair article needs a different alt.
blocks.12.text: When an empty alt is right
blocks.13.text: There is one case: the image is pure decoration and carries no information. A flourish, a divider, a background pattern. Then alt="" is a deliberate message to the reader: "skip this, there is nothing here for you". The difference is that you decided it, rather than forgot it.
blocks.15.text: Above you see exactly that: two broken paths, yet the page still says what was on them. That is the difference.
blocks.16.text: Your turn. Restore the correct paths (/uroci/bike.jpg and /uroci/bike2.jpg) and give each image an alt that really says what is on it.
